question: Your migration is one line: ALTER TABLE orders RENAME COLUMN ship_to TO shipping_address. It runs in microseconds. The deploy is a rolling update across 6 instances and completes in 90 seconds. What is the total damage?
options: None — the rename is atomic and the rollout is short, so at worst a handful of requests see a transient error
Some 5xx errors during the 90-second window, which stop as soon as the last instance is on the new version and leave nothing behind

5xx errors from old code writing the old column, AND silently wrong data from old code reading it: a missing column raises on the write path but returns None on the read path, so anything that persists a read result becomes permanent corruption
Only read errors — writes are fine because the database maps the old column name to the new one for backward compatibility
explanation: Measured over 1,700 requests through the mixed fleet: 202 requests raised OperationalError (loud, self-healing) and 90 of 363 exported rows persisted a NULL address (silent, permanent). The read path in every ORM is SELECT * followed by a dictionary lookup, and a missing key yields None rather than an exception. The 5xxs stop when the rollout finishes; the 90 corrupt rows are found by reconciliation the next morning.

question: An ALTER TABLE whose own work takes 1 ms is queued behind a 3-second analytics SELECT. Ordinary SELECTs keep arriving. What happens to those ordinary SELECTs, which do not conflict with the analytics query at all?
options: They run normally — ACCESS SHARE does not conflict with ACCESS SHARE, and the ALTER is not holding any lock yet
They queue behind the waiting ALTER, because a lock is granted only if it conflicts with nothing HELD and nothing already WAITING ahead of it — so a DDL that holds nothing still blocks the table
They are cancelled immediately with a lock-not-available error and must be retried by the client
They run, but at reduced priority, because PostgreSQL de-prioritises readers while a DDL is pending
explanation: This is the lock-queue cascade. The waiting ALTER is ahead of them in the queue and conflicts with them, so they wait — 42 of them in the measured run, worst wait 2,461 ms, 51.7 query-seconds of stalled traffic, all caused by a statement whose own work was 1 ms and which held no lock at all. Setting lock_timeout to 50 ms with a jittered retry cut it to 4 blocked queries and 0.08 query-seconds; each abort drains the queue that formed behind you.

question: You need to backfill a new column across 1,000,000 rows. One UPDATE finishes in 2.43 s; 50 bounded batches take 4.28 s. Which do you ship to production, and why?
options: The single UPDATE — it is 76% faster and holds the lock for less total time, so the disruption window is shorter

The batched version — the extra wall time buys a 21x shorter worst-case lock hold (2,427 ms to 114 ms), and it avoids pinning one MVCC snapshot open for the whole run, which would stop vacuum reclaiming dead rows database-wide
The single UPDATE, wrapped in a SERIALIZABLE transaction so concurrent readers see a consistent snapshot throughout
Either one — the total row-locking work is identical, so the choice is a matter of style
explanation: Batching is genuinely slower on wall time, and that is the trade you are choosing. In the measured run a concurrent reader issuing one small indexed SELECT every 2 ms completed exactly 1 query during the 2.43-second single transaction (that query took 2,426 ms) versus 165 queries during the 4.28-second batched run, p50 0.1 ms. A single long transaction also holds a snapshot open, so vacuum cannot reclaim any dead row version anywhere while it runs, and the resulting bloat outlives the migration.

question: You have deployed the expand migration (new nullable column added) and the dual-writing code. The backfill is still running. A teammate wants to ship the deploy that switches readers to the new column now, to save a release cycle. What happens?
options: Nothing bad — new writes populate both columns, so any row a reader touches will have the new column filled in
Reads of rows the backfill has not reached yet return NULL with no exception raised, which is the same silent corruption as the naive rename, just on a schedule you chose
The reads fail loudly with a not-null violation, so the problem is caught immediately by the error rate
The database transparently falls back to the old column until the backfill completes
explanation: In the rollback matrix, state S1 (expanded but not backfilled) scores SILENT for both v2r and v2 — no exception, wrong answer. Only rows written since the dual-write deploy have the new column populated; every legacy row the backfill has not reached still holds NULL. Gate the reader deploy on a reconciliation query that counts rows where the two shapes disagree and requires zero. If you cannot write that query, you do not know the backfill finished.

question: Which step in an expand / migrate / contract sequence actually forfeits your ability to roll back, and what does the failure look like if you roll back past it?
options: The expand migration, because adding a column changes the table's physical layout and old code cannot parse the new rows
The backfill, because once historical rows are rewritten the original values are gone

The contract (the DROP), because afterwards only the newest code version works — the older versions raise on every single request rather than degrading, and re-adding the column does not bring the data back
The reader switch, because once traffic reads the new column the old one goes stale and can no longer be trusted
explanation: Running every code version against every schema state gives the answer directly: 1 of 4 versions work before expand, 2 of 4 after expand, all 4 after the backfill — the safest moment in the whole sequence — and only 1 of 4 after the contract. The DROP is the one irreversible step, which is why it ships last, alone, after a real soak, and why the deploy that stops writing the old shape must be separate from the migration that removes it.

question: A migration must run before its code deploy, and it is not backward compatible with the version currently running in production. What is the correct response?
options: Run the migration during the deploy and keep the rollout window as short as possible, so the incompatibility lasts only seconds
Run the migration inside the same transaction as the deploy so that both apply atomically across the fleet
Deploy the new code first and run the migration immediately afterwards, so the new code is already in place when the schema changes

Recognise that it is a migration requiring downtime, and either split it into steps that are each backward compatible or schedule an announced maintenance window — no ordering trick removes the incompatibility
explanation: There is no ordering that makes an incompatible change safe, because there is no moment at which only one version of your code is running: rollouts are gradual, rollbacks reintroduce the old version, and cron boxes, batch workers and mobile clients are not in the rollout at all. Either restage it as expand/migrate/contract steps that are each compatible in both directions, or take the outage deliberately. A planned two-minute window at 04:00 with the fleet drained is a far better outcome than an unplanned four-minute one at 15:40, and the option only exists if someone named it in review.
